subject: ap-chemistry
title: Metal-Air Electrochemical Cells
stimulus:
Metal-air cells are a relatively new type of portable energy source consisting of a metal anode, an alkaline
electrolyte paste that contains water, and a porous cathode membrane that lets in oxygen from the air. A
schematic of the cell is shown above. Reduction potentials for the cathode and three possible metal anodes are
given in the table below.
Half Reaction E at pH 11 and 298 K (V)
$O_2$(g) + 2 $H_2O$(l) + 4 e$^-$ → 4 OH$^-$(aq) +0.34
ZnO(s) + $H_2O$(l) + 2 e$^-$ → Zn(s) + 2 OH$^-$(aq) –1.31
$Na_2O$(s) + $H_2O$(l) + 2 e$^-$ → 2 Na(s) + 2 OH$^-$(aq) –1.60
CaO(s) + $H_2O$(l) + 2 e$^-$ → Ca(s) + 2 OH$^-$(aq) –2.78

parts.prompt:
Early forms of metal-air cells used zinc as the anode. Zinc oxide is produced as the cell operates according
to the overall equation below.
2 Zn(s) + $O_2$(g) → 2 ZnO(s)
(i) Using the data in the table above, calculate the cell potential for the zinc-air cell.
(ii) The electrolyte paste contains OH$^-$ ions. On the diagram of the cell above, draw an arrow to indicate
the direction of migration of OH$^-$ ions through the electrolyte as the cell operates.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (a)(i): The cathode is O$_2$ reduction (+0.34 V) and the anode is Zn oxidation (reverse of ZnO/Zn half-reaction: +1.31 V). $E°_{cell} = 0.34 - (-1.31) = +1.65$ V. Part (a)(ii): OH$^-$ ions migrate toward the anode (Zn electrode) through the electrolyte to complete the circuit — anions migrate toward the anode in a galvanic cell.

parts.prompt:
A fresh zinc-air cell is weighed on an analytical balance before being placed in a hearing aid for use.
(i) As the cell operates, does the mass of the cell increase, decrease, or remain the same?
(ii) Justify your answer to part (b)(i) in terms of the equation for the overall cell reaction.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): Increases. Part (b)(ii): The overall reaction 2 Zn(s) + O$_2$(g) → 2 ZnO(s) consumes O$_2$ from the surrounding air. This O$_2$ has mass that becomes incorporated into the ZnO product inside the cell, increasing the cell's total mass.

alternatives.correct_example: Increases. O$_2$ from the surrounding air is incorporated into the cell as a reactant.

wrong_examples: Decreases or remains the same — the cell gains the mass of O$_2$ consumed from outside air.
common_traps: The cell is open to air; O$_2$ enters from outside and becomes part of the ZnO product, adding mass.

scoring_points.description: For correct justification referencing O$_2$ from air entering the cell: 1 point

required_elements: The overall reaction 2 Zn(s) + O$_2$(g) → 2 ZnO(s) consumes O$_2$ gas from the surrounding air.
The mass of the O$_2$ from the air is added to the cell as ZnO.
alternatives.correct_example: According to 2 Zn(s) + O$_2$(g) → 2 ZnO(s), molecular oxygen from the atmosphere is consumed and incorporated into the zinc oxide product. The O$_2$ was external to the cell originally but becomes part of ZnO inside the cell, increasing the total mass.

wrong_examples: Saying the cell produces gas that escapes — the reaction produces a solid (ZnO), which stays in the cell.
common_traps: The reaction product ZnO stays in the cell; the O$_2$ reactant comes from outside. Net result: the cell gains the mass of O$_2$.

parts.prompt:
The zinc-air cell is taken to the top of a mountain where the air pressure is lower.
(i) Will the cell potential be higher, lower, or the same as the cell potential at the lower elevation?
(ii) Justify your answer to part (c)(i) based on the equation for the overall cell reaction and the information
above.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (c)(i): Lower. Part (c)(ii): At high altitude, partial pressure of O$_2$ is reduced. O$_2$ is a reactant in the cathode half-reaction. By the Nernst equation, lower P(O$_2$) increases Q and decreases the cell potential.

alternatives.correct_example: Lower. At high altitude, the partial pressure of O$_2$ in the air is reduced.

wrong_examples: Higher or the same — reduced O$_2$ pressure shifts the equilibrium away from products, lowering the cell potential.
common_traps: Lower P(O$_2$) → Q increases for the cell reaction → $E_{cell} < E°_{cell}$ (Nernst equation).

scoring_points.description: For justification referencing reduced O$_2$ partial pressure: 1 point

required_elements: At higher elevation, the partial pressure of O$_2$ is reduced.
Lower P(O$_2$) decreases the driving force for the cathode reaction (less O$_2$ available for reduction), shifting Q and reducing $E_{cell}$.
alternatives.correct_example: At the mountaintop, atmospheric pressure is lower, so the partial pressure of O$_2$ is reduced. O$_2$ is a reactant in the overall reaction. By the Nernst equation, $E = E° - (RT/nF)\ln Q$; lower P(O$_2$) increases Q (fewer reactants), which decreases the cell potential below $E°$.

wrong_examples: Referencing temperature — the question specifies lower air pressure at altitude, not lower temperature.
common_traps: The cell reaction requires O$_2$ as a reactant. Any decrease in O$_2$ concentration/pressure reduces the thermodynamic driving force.

parts.prompt:
Metal-air cells need to be lightweight for many applications. In order to transfer more electrons with a
smaller mass, Na and Ca are investigated as potential anodes. A 1.0 g anode of which of these metals
would transfer more electrons, assuming that the anode is totally consumed during the lifetime of a cell?
Justify your answer with calculations.
parts.scoring_notes: Na (1 g): $n = 1.0/22.99 = 0.0435$ mol. Na$_2$O anode half-reaction: 2 Na → Na$_2$O + 2e$^-$ (or Na$_2$O + H$_2$O + 2e$^-$ → 2 Na + 2OH$^-$ reversed). Electrons per mol Na = 1 e$^-$/Na atom → 0.0435 mol e$^-$. Ca (1 g): $n = 1.0/40.08 = 0.0249$ mol. Ca → Ca$^{2+}$ + 2e$^-$, so 0.0499 mol e$^-$. Wait — Na: 1 e$^-$ per atom, Ca: 2 e$^-$ per atom. Na: 0.0435 mol × 1 = 0.0435 mol e$^-$; Ca: 0.0249 mol × 2 = 0.0499 mol e$^-$. Ca transfers more electrons per gram.

scoring_points.description: For correct calculations comparing electrons transferred per gram for Na and Ca: 1 point

required_elements: Na: $(1.0\text{ g}/22.99\text{ g/mol}) \times 1\text{ mol e}^-/\text{mol Na} = 0.043\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca: $(1.0\text{ g}/40.08\text{ g/mol}) \times 2\text{ mol e}^-/\text{mol Ca} = 0.050\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca transfers more electrons per gram of anode.
alternatives.correct_example:
Na (1 e$^-$ per atom): $1.0\text{ g} / 22.99\text{ g/mol} = 0.043\text{ mol Na} \times 1 = 0.043\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca (2 e$^-$ per atom): $1.0\text{ g} / 40.08\text{ g/mol} = 0.025\text{ mol Ca} \times 2 = 0.050\text{ mol e}^-$
Ca transfers more electrons per gram.

wrong_examples: Saying Na transfers more — Na has a smaller molar mass but only 1 e$^-$ per atom; Ca has 2 e$^-$ per atom which compensates for its greater mass.
common_traps: Electrons transferred per gram = (1/M) × (e$^-$ per atom). Must account for both molar mass and oxidation state.

parts.prompt:
The only common oxide of zinc has the formula ZnO.
(i) Write the electron configuration for a Zn atom in the ground state.
(ii) From which sublevel are electrons removed when a Zn atom in the ground state is oxidized?
parts.scoring_notes: Part (e)(i): [Ar] 3d$^{10}$ 4s$^2$ (or full: 1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ 3s$^2$ 3p$^6$ 3d$^{10}$ 4s$^2$). Part (e)(ii): 4s. Electrons are removed from the highest-energy occupied subshell. For Zn, the 4s is higher in energy (outermost principal quantum number) than 3d, so the two 4s electrons are removed first when Zn is oxidized to Zn$^{2+}$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct ground-state electron configuration of Zn: 1 point
wrong_examples: [Ar] 4s$^2$ 3d$^9$ — Zn has a completely filled 3d subshell.
common_traps: Zn (Z = 30) has a completely filled 3d$^{10}$ subshell plus 4s$^2$. Not to be confused with transition metals that have partially filled d subshells.

alternatives.correct_example: The 4s sublevel. When Zn is oxidized to Zn$^{2+}$, both electrons are removed from the 4s subshell. The 4s electrons are in the highest principal energy level (n = 4) and are removed first.

wrong_examples: 3d — while 3d is higher in energy than 4s for free atoms in some contexts, for transition metals and Zn the 4s electrons are removed first upon ionization.
common_traps: Electrons are removed in the reverse order of filling for ionization of d-block metals: 4s before 3d.
